data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Early Deals And Generative AI Push Holiday Spending To Nearly $80B
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Consumers have already spent about $79.7 billion online this holiday season. (0:15) White House green lights ‘Rush Hour 4.’ (1:50) OpenAI forecasts 220 million paying for ChatGPT. (2:13)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, consumers have already spent about $79.7 billion online this holiday season. Adobe says the early holiday spending is up about 7.5% from last year's pace.

Adobe highlighted that the strong spending so far has been driven in part by early deals. For the first 23 days of the season, discounts for electronics peaked at 12% but are expected to jump to 29% on Cyber Monday. In other categories, Cyber Monday is set to have the biggest discounts for apparel at 25% and computers at 23%.

Meanwhile, Black Friday is set to have the biggest discounts for televisions at 23%. Toys and appliances are also forecast to see big discounts.

The data from Adobe indicated that shoppers are also trading up to higher-ticket items, taking advantage of deals to get greater value.

Generative AI is also a major holiday shopping factor. In the first 23 days of the season, AI traffic rose by 830% year over year. The generative AI tools were noted to have been used most for categories including toys, video games, appliances, personal care products and electronics.

Adobe also observed that shoppers who landed on a U.S. retail site from an AI service were 30x more likely to buy something. Additionally, 32% of respondents reported having used AI to aid in their online shopping journeys, and nearly half said they have used or plan to use AI specifically for holiday shopping.

Among active stocks, led by strength in its namesake banner and its digital channel, Urban Outfitters (URBN) reached record sales and record net income for Q3, beating expectations and driving shares sharply higher.

Autodesk (ADSK) is higher after the design software maker reported a quarterly beat and outlined a $7.15 billion–$7.165 billion revenue target for 2026 as platform and AI momentum grow.

And Workday (WDAY) is slumping after analysts cited investor concerns over current remaining performance obligations growth being driven by M&A.

In other news of note, Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is reviving the “Rush Hour” movie series, nearly two decades after the last installment – with a push from the White House.

President Donald Trump "personally pressed" Paramount to revive the buddy-cop comedy movie series, starring Jackie Chan and Chris Tucker, Semafor reported this week.

Paramount will be paid a flat distribution fee to release “Rush Hour 4” theatrically, Variety reported. Warner Bros. (WBD), whose New Line Cinema label backed the previous Rush Hour films, will get first-dollar gross.

OpenAI has projected that at least 220 million of ChatGPT's weekly users will pay for a subscription.

According to The Information, OpenAI (OPENAI) projects that by 2030, 8.5% of an estimated 2.6 billion weekly users, or around 220 million people, will subscribe to its chatbot, positioning ChatGPT among the world's largest subscription businesses.

As of July, about 35 million users, which is roughly 5% of ChatGPT's weekly active base, paid for "Plus" or "Pro" plans at $20 and $200 a month, respectively.

And Campbell's (CPB) has defended the ingredients in its soups and placed one of its executives on leave after a lawsuit highlighted his alleged derogatory comments in an audio recording.

The employment discrimination lawsuit was filed by former cybersecurity analyst Robert Garza. According to the suit, Garza had a meeting with Martin Bally, Campbell's vice president of IT, which he recorded.

Bally allegedly described Campbell's as highly processed food for "poor people," and made several racist comments about the company's Indian workers.

"I don't buy Campbell's products barely anymore. It's not healthy now that I know what the f—'s in it," Bally claimed. He also allegedly referenced "bioengineered" meat, saying, "I don't wanna eat a piece of chicken that came from a 3-D printer."

On that note, maybe stick to Turkey tomorrow. Have a safe and happy Thanksgiving.

data.title: As The Market Shrugs Off Another Criteo Earnings Beat, Contrarians Should Take Note
data.description:
Among Internet advertisers, Criteo S.A. (CRTO) has, shall we say, a checkered reputation. In 2017 Apple made a big deal about Criteo’s use of browser cookies to target ads across different platforms. In 2023, they were hit by a €40 million fine from the French privacy watchdog, CNIL.

Criteo has plummeted over scandal numerous times, and somehow always bounced back. Today though, the market seems largely pessimistic about them, shrugging off a Q3 beat that not only came in solidly ahead of EPS, but hugely ahead of revenue estimates. Seemingly for years this company could survive anything and the market forgave them for missteps rather quickly. The market sentiment today, even after the beat, seems to suggest they view it as a company that can do no right.

When a stock price gives me the impression they are ignoring the actual numbers, that’s when the contrarian in me starts getting curious, so I thought this was a good time to have a deeper look at the business and ask whether the market is right in dismissing earnings beats that are becoming more and more common, or if investors have been sleeping on a possible turnaround candidate.

Criteo, But Retail-Oriented

Once upon a time, Criteo was an Internet advertiser locked into the web browser. Today, the company is trying a more diverse strategy, aiming for partnerships with retail brands. According to the most recent conference call, 85% of their media spending is outside the desktop display segment that they were once locked into. Indeed, 50% of their spending is focused on retail media.

What does all that mean? Criteo went from the familiar display ads in the web browser on the computer model to a broader commerce-media platform. That is to say, they help retail customers sell sponsored listings and other ads for shoppers inside their own storefronts. They've also got a substantial data catalogue that helps brands target shoppers. Instead of web-retargeting browser ads were once the focus, Criteo is now shaping its main focus as an infrastructure provider in retail, which if we're being honest is historically higher margin than what they used to focus on. That's key, as we'll soon see.

The once French company is also moving to Luxembourg, and moving from the ADS model to listing the ordinary shares directly on the NASDAQ. This was done to increase their potential for inclusion in US indices. But what does the company’s balance sheet look like?

Cash and Equivalents

$255 million

Total Current Assets

$1.00 billion

Total Current Liabilities

$755 million

Total Shareholder Equities

$1.13 billion

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

This is the first noteworthy thing for contrarian investors – Criteo has a decent balance sheet, a respectable current ratio of 1.32, which isn’t great, but is fine for a profitable company. Best of all, the price/book ratio is 0.93, which is well below the sector median of 1.71.

The assets seem somewhat undervalued, but by itself, that doesn’t mean a lot. This is where we can start looking at their earnings.

Persistent Beats Suggest Low Estimates

Criteo came in not just ahead of estimates in Q3 but substantially so. The 70¢ EPS being 25¢ ahead of what we were expecting is something, but the revenue estimate was $281.57 million and the 10-Q shows they came in with $469.66 million. That level of beat just doesn’t happen unless the estimates are outdated or profoundly skewed toward pessimism. It’s unclear which is the case here, but Criteo has persistently beat estimates, with 11 quarters straight exceeding expectations. The estimates projected a Criteo with sharply declining revenue and earnings weakness, what we have instead is this.

2023

2024

2025 (Q1-Q3)

Revenue

$1.95 billion

$1.93 billion

$1.40 billion

Gross Profit

$863 million

$983 million

$752 million

Gross Margin

44.3%

50.9%

53.6%

Operating Income

$77 million

$151 million

$130 million

Operating Margin

3.96%

7.83%

9.28%

Net Income

$53 million

$112 million

$97 million

Diluted GAAP EPS

88¢

$1.90

$1.75

Non-GAAP EPS

$3.18

$4.57

$3.33

(source: most recent 10-K and 10-Q from SEC)

Revenue is not cratering, it was trivially down in 2023 and year over year it’s actually up slightly in 2025 ($1.40 billion vs. $1.38 billion for the first three quarters in 2024). The estimates are still that revenue would be down, with only $1.17 billion for the full 2025 year, which Criteo has already blown past with a whole quarter to go.

Then there’s the earnings and we come across the most underappreciated aspect of Criteo for the past two years, the improving margins. We saw the margins increase an impressive amount in 2024, and continue to trend upward in 2025, even as the revenue remained largely flat.

The GAAP P/E for 2024 is 10.48, which beats the sector median of 17.12 comfortably. The non-GAAP P/E is where Criteo really shines though, coming in at 4.36 compared to the sector’s 12.73. The estimate for Q4 is $1.41, and even assuming that Criteo doesn’t manage to beat the estimates once again, that gives us a full year of $4.74, meaning a forward P/E of 4.20 against the sector’s more modest 14.69.

Purely by the numbers, Criteo seems rather exciting. The estimates continue to predict a revenue fall-off, but the company is instead setting the stage to grow their consumer base on multiple fronts as it moves to reinvent itself in a rapidly changing market. So let’s look into that next.

Google and DoorDash: Criteo’s Bid for Growth

While the company seems more than a little successful in continuing to earn, they’re not content to rest on their laurels. This past quarter saw Criteo make a couple of major moves.

The first is integration into the Google API. This allows Google ad customers, the leviathan that they are, to access Criteo’s ever-growing retailer inventory. That integration includes unified analytics.

It’s good for Google customers, because it gives them new options that plug right into the existing analytics. It’s even better news for Criteo’s retailers, who suddenly get a massive amount of new exposure by being on Google’s ad options, which should make it easier for Criteo to attract more clients on the retail front.

Criteo isn’t stopping with Google. In the conference call they noted they’re testing a similar arrangement with Microsoft in Q4, which should make Criteo an even better option for retailers.

Also reflecting Criteo’s effort to grow the retailer stream, last month they signed a partnership deal with DoorDash. Criteo described it as them serving “as an extension of DoorDash’s US ad sales team” with respect to non-restaurant retailers.

It also gives Criteo advertisers access to the existing DoorDash ad formats, which is potentially a valuable new option for them, and should give Criteo more momentum on the retail front.

These reflect Criteo’s effort to grow with bigger players, but at the same time, the company has brought in an AI agent option to make smaller offerings with more self-service. This lowers costs for Criteo and allows them to offer entry-level service that previously wouldn’t have been economical. The use of self-service may in part be the reason why their margins are improving. Revenue is on the rise in 2025, but the all-important traffic acquisition costs are meaningfully declining.

A Reward But Also a Risk

New partners are a great opportunity for Criteo to strengthen their game, but it also makes them dependent on third parties for portions of their business in a way their old Internet model wasn’t. If Google, for instance, changes their fees, suddenly it could matter a lot more to Criteo than it used to.

But the more serious risk I see is a macroeconomic one. Criteo is doing well, and retail advertising is surprisingly robust, but in a more protracted market downturn we could see companies reducing marketing budgets, and Criteo will have to chase those customers for what money they are spending. That poses a risk for both revenue and margins, which remain a strong suit when we’re assessing the company.

Finally, we can’t sleep on regulatory risks. In the past, Criteo has gotten hit by regulators over data practices, and the advertising industry is a place where rules are always changing, and Criteo in particular would be treated with suspicion because of past times they got punished for being non-compliant. The hope is they’ve learned their lesson, but we never really know for certain.

A Contrarian Play Worth Taking an Interest In

While their past is somewhat unfortunate, I believe Criteo is doing a good job moving into the retail sector and away from their past efforts as one of many desktop display advertising agencies. Given their numbers and their tendency to beat estimates, I’m rating them a buy.

Investors holding Criteo should maintain a close eye on their revenue as well as on their traffic acquisition costs. Those costs are a good metric of how efficient the company is being, and if they start to go up without an accompanying revenue growth, that may be a sign of trouble.

Criteo has a complicated history, but the numbers suggest they’re worth looking into for contrarian investors. There is a lot to like here, and the market at large doesn’t seem to appreciate that yet.

data.title: Ovintiv: Another Good Play In The Permian Basin, Adding Promising M&A Deals
data.description:
Ovintiv (OVV) is a company dedicated to the exploration, extraction, and production of oil, LNG, and natural gas in North America. The company operates in Canada, specifically in the Montney Basin, and in the United States, exploiting its holdings in the Permian Basin, east of Texas.

Due to the drop in international oil prices, the company has experienced a decline in its results. Even so, it announced in early November the completion of its acquisition of NuVista Energy. This would provide Ovintiv with an expansion of its acreage in Canada, adding to its production, which continues to increase despite the drop in selling prices. Based on the information presented in this report, and given the valuation I performed for Ovintiv, a purchase opportunity can be seen considering these factors and its positioning in both the American and Canadian markets.

Thesis

Ovintiv has demonstrated its ability to maintain high levels of cash flow generation, even despite the drop in international oil prices and declining margins. As I mentioned previously, OVV divides its business model into the exploitation of two distinct basins. In the United States, the company operates in the Permian Basin, specifically from its holdings in East Texas, with a presence of approximately four to five drilling rigs. Meanwhile, its existing portfolio in Canada, specifically in the Montney Basin, was expanded through the acquisition of NuVista Energy, with a projected presence of three to four rigs.

As can be seen below, the company operates in the most productive basins in North America, with its presence in the Permian Basin and its expansion in the Montney Basin. In this sense, the acquisition of NuVista Energy is a strategic decision, as the company also seeks to divest its assets in the Anadarko Basin to concentrate on its most productive areas.

}One of the interesting aspects of this particular company, when compared to its main competitors in the Permian Basin, is its presence in Canada. Initially, one might expect that the selling price of oil extracted from Montney would differ from the price of WTI. However, far from this scenario, the price of Montney Condesate (Condy) maintains an arbitrage opportunity with an extremely strong correlation to WTI, given the proximity of the main extraction sites. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that, beyond any differences in the FX market, the prices at which the company operates are similar in Canada and the US.

Now, one might ask what kind of advantage this presence offers in the Canadian basins compared to a company operating entirely in the Permian Basin. The key lies in the royalties paid in Canada compared to the United States. While royalty rates in US basins fluctuate between 20% and 25% regardless of the final sale price, this is not the case in Canada. In Canada, the rate depends on the price of a barrel of crude oil in international markets. As the price falls, the royalty percentage also decreases. It can even be observed that only when WTI reaches $135 do Canadian royalties become comparable to those in the US.

If we add to this the company's expansion in Canada following the consolidation of NuVista Energy, we see potential compared to other competitors. The deal, which was confirmed on November 4, is valued at $2.7 billion. According to OVV, the acquisition of the remaining shares of NuVista Energy was completed with a 50% cash payment and the remaining 50% paid in company stock. The company paid the equivalent of CAD 18 per NuVista share. However, since OVV already held a 9.6% stake in the company, the effective price of the transaction was CAD 17.80 per share.

Ovintiv reported that they expect to be fully operational by 2026, developing the approximately 140,000 net acres acquired in the deal, which would add the equivalent of 100 MBOE/d. ​​Simultaneously with this acquisition, and to improve the company's productivity, Ovintiv announced a divestment plan for its Andaranko assets. This will allow them to recoup some of their investment and focus on developing their operations in Canada and the United States.

Given this scenario, it's clear the company maintains a growth outlook and potential for improved margins as it focuses on developing the most productive areas on the continent. Simultaneously, repurposing some of the cultivated land could yield benefits in terms of royalties, boosting overall results. As we'll see later, this is further supported by a valuation that, based on the models I've developed, is below the current market price. Therefore, I rate OVV as a BUY.

3Q2025 Results

In this section, I would like to summarize the main results reported by the company in Q3 2025 and compare them with previous periods to gain a deeper understanding of the company's current situation. To begin, I would like to mention that during this quarter, OVV reported production of 630 MBOE/d, which is approaching the highest estimates management had made for 2025. This 630 MBOE/d breaks down into approximately 212,000 barrels of crude oil per day, 98 Mbbls/d of other NGLs, and 1.925 million cubic feet per day of natural gas.

Regarding the results themselves, the company reported revenues of $2.02 billion. Compared to previous periods, this represents a slight decrease from the $2.214 billion of Q2 2025 and a 7.25% drop from the $2.178 billion of the same period last year. Considering that the average selling price of a barrel of oil fell from $75.09 to $64.93 this year, the reason for this decrease in revenue becomes clear.

Turning to EBITDA, the company reported a value of $966 million for Q3 2025. This again represents a decrease compared to the values ​​of the previous quarter and the third quarter of 2024. In the first case, the amount reported by OVV was $1,076 million, while in Q3 2024 it was $1,131 million, implying a 14.6% drop. Regarding margins, we also see a slight deterioration from one year to the next. While the 2024 EBITDA margin was around 51.93%, for 2025 the value is around 47.82%. However, when we annualize the company's EBITDA value and compare it to the current EV, we see that the EV/EBITDA ratio is around 4.21x, which is extremely interesting from my point of view.

Another point I'd like to highlight and analyze is the Cash from Operations that the company reported. The value for Q3 2024 was $812 million. While the trend compared to previous years is also downward, I find it interesting to emphasize the ratio obtained when compared to the market cap. If we annualize this latter value, OVV's P/CF ratio is around 3.03x. The fact that a company can generate enough cash in approximately three years to cover its market cap is, in my opinion, extremely interesting and attractive.

The last point I'd like to mention is the debt reduction the company has been undertaking recently. Since the first quarter of 2024, with the sole exception of a rebound in Q1 2025, the company has embarked on a path of debt reduction that, in my view, is healthy. In fact, considering the latest interest payments of $91 million in the quarter and comparing them to the company's EBITDA, we can see that the EBITDA/interest ratio stands at a healthy 10.6x. Therefore, from my perspective, debt is not a concern, especially given management's commitment to leveraging free cash flow generation to further deleverage the capital structure.

Valuation

In this section, I would like to present a valuation I made of the company using two methods. Before delving into the details of each valuation, the assumptions used, and the results obtained, I would like to point out that, from my perspective, they are interesting insofar as they both show similar upside potential.

To begin the aforementioned analysis and perform an OVV valuation, I would like to provide details of the DCF model I developed. In this regard, it is important to outline the assumptions I used in developing the model. First, regarding the tax rate, I used a value of 20%, which is in line with the average of recent years, as can be seen in the Financials section of Seeking Alpha. As for the perpetual growth rate, I chose a value of 2%. This is below the average global GDP growth rate. The objective of using a value with lower long-term growth potential was to build a margin of safety in the results obtained and make the baseline scenario I am working with rather conservative.

Before discussing EBIT growth and the other parameters that make up the company's projected FCF, I'd like to mention the discount rate used. In this case, I decided to use a WACC of 10%. To analyze this value and see that, again, it's constructed while maintaining a safety margin, it's necessary to understand the cost of debt and equity. OVV's Kd is around 5.27% if we look at the yield of the bonds issued by the company and weight them by volume.

If we analyze the capital structure according to the company's market cap and debt value, we see that equity currently represents 60.5% of total assets and debt the remaining 39.5%. Under this distribution, with a Ke of approximately 13%, we obtain a WACC of 10%. Furthermore, considering that the company's beta is around 0.79, we can see that a Ke of approximately 13% does indeed provide a significant margin of safety.

In fact, consulting specialized sites shows that the assumption I used of 10% is more than enough to discount the cash flows with the desired safety margin, considering that they place the WACC at around 7.4%.

Now, I would like to analyze EBIT growth and the remaining values ​​that affect final FCF. First, considering the scenario described in the thesis section and based on market specialists' earnings growth projections, I estimated an 8% increase in EBITDA for the first year. In subsequent periods, the increases would be 4%, 3.5%, and 2.5% for the last two years, eventually converging to the perpetual growth rate. To simplify the calculations, I assumed that the increase in depreciation and amortization would be in line with the aforementioned EBITDA growth. Finally, regarding CAPEX, I estimated a steady increase of 1.5% based on the latest reported annualized figure.

Under these assumptions, it can be seen that the company presents good upside potential, which is in line with the scenario described in the previous sections. However, to further strengthen the analysis, I would also like to present a valuation of the company using multiples.

For this particular study, I decided to use as comparables some of the companies listed in the Peers segment of Seeking Alpha. All of them belong to the oil and gas industry with a presence in the Permian Basin and have a similar market capitalization. In this regard, I decided to use the P/E ratio, EV/EBITDA, and P/CF as multiples for the comparison. In each case, I calculated the average ratio of the selected companies, weighted by their market capitalization. Once this was done, I was able to obtain a target price for OVV based on each ratio. Finally, using the weights shown in the following table, I obtained an average price. The first thing that stands out is that the upside potential generated by this method is of a similar magnitude to that obtained by DCF.

Finally, the second part of the table shows a section where I have summarized a series of financial and economic indicators that I considered relevant for comparing OVV with its competitors. As the table shows, the company is in an interesting position, surpassed only by PR and APA.

Risks

Before concluding this report, I would like to briefly mention the main risks I can identify in the short term. These warnings are primarily linked to the changes in the tariff structure that the Trump administration intends to implement and the escalation toward a potential trade conflict with China. As the world's two largest economies, a tariff war between the United States and China could not only increase market volatility but also slow global GDP growth.

At the same time, it is important to consider that one of the main victims of the new scheme proposed by the White House is the Asian giant. Furthermore, given that China is one of the largest consumers of energy-related commodities, a slowdown in its economic growth could lead to a drop in oil and gas demand, which would put downward pressure on international prices.

Undoubtedly, the trend of recent years regarding WTI could lead to a decrease in OVV's results that negatively impacts the share price.

Conclusion

Based on everything we've seen, and despite the drop in international oil prices that impacted the company's revenue, OVV has interesting upside potential. On the one hand, the acquisition of NuVista Energy is a play that would imply a significant increase in production. On the other hand, Ovintiv shows strong cash flow generation and a number of very attractive ratios, especially P/CF. If we add to that a manageable debt level and a valuation that shows the company is trading below its potential, the reasons for my BUY rating become clear.

data.title: GPIQ: Simplifying The Game For Retirees Seeking Income Generation
data.description:
Overview

I believe that this is one of the greatest times in modern history to be an income investor because of all the funds available now. When retirees wanted to collect a double digit yield a decade ago, they were often limited to higher risk funds that utilized a range of different debt securities, which were typically sensitive to things like interest rates and credit defaults. Strategic option funds like the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) have changed the game for retirees. When I previously covered GPIQ, I discussed the differences compared to a higher yield alternative. However, I wanted to revisit the fund to discuss how GPIQ can be great for retired investors seeking stability.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that GPIQ's share price has increased by about 4.3%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in market rallies over time, despite its limitations. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 15.8% over the same time frame. GPIQ now offers investors a trailing twelve month distribution rate of about 9.4% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. This makes the fund an enticing choice for investors seeking frequent income generation within a diverse portfolio.

I like the fact that GPIQ keeps investors exposed to some of the highest quality companies in the world. Instead of taking on elevated levels of risk, GPIQ can provide a near double-digit yield without exposing investors to many of the vulnerabilities that other income funds may have. Additionally, GPIQ has demonstrated its ability to keep payouts within a consistent range, while also prioritizing tax-efficiency. An investor with a large portfolio can easily create a sizeable dividend income that can create a higher quality retirement. While there are some risks, I believe that the benefits outweigh these concerns. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that GPIQ implements to generate its earnings.

Beneficial Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, GPIQ has total net assets of $2.07B that is spread across 108 different holdings. The fund has a gross expense ratio of 0.35% with an inception dating back to October of 2023. The fund essentially provides exposure to constituents of the Nasdaq-100 index, while deploying a dynamic option writing strategy to amplify the income that can be collected from holdings. This dynamic option writing approach is the standout feature of the fund because it allows GPIQ to participate in the capital appreciation of its holdings. In short, this dynamic option writing works in the following way:

  1. Management may increase the rate of written options during choppy or declining markets, which can help offset lower returns.
  2. Management may decrease the rate of written options during strong bull markets. This helps the fund participate in a greater level of capital appreciation over time.

Since GPIQ is made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100 index, the fund's breakdown by sector comes as no surprise. As we can see below, GPIQ is most heavily allocated to Technology companies at 54.53% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Communication and Consumer Cyclical investments, which accounts for 16.55% and 12.74% of assets respectively. Since the premiums collected from options are higher during periods of elevated implied volatility, the greater allocation to technology stocks works in GPIQ's favor.

Since GPIQ holds traditional equities without the inclusion of any other debt securities, the fund's vulnerability to things like interest rate changes or defaults are irrelevant. The only tradeoff is that the option strategy still caps the upside potential to whatever the selected strike price is. Despite this weakness, the strategy essentially allows investors to ride the momentum of the market over time, making it a less stressful position within a diversified portfolio.

Since GPIQ is made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100, the fund provides exposure to some of the most innovative and highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest position at 10.3% of assets. This is followed by exposure to other notable companies such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), and Amazon (AMZN), just to name a few.

Performance Strengths & Weaknesses

Since the inclusion of an option writing strategy means that the growth potential is capped, GPIQ is very likely to underperform a more traditional index-tracking ETF over time, such as the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). Just to demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance chart of GPIQ since its inception. As we can see below, GPIQ's total return sits around 62.9%, which underperforms compared to QQQ's total return of 73.8% over the same time frame. Therefore, I want to emphasize that the higher dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a higher total return over time.

However, I want to point out that despite the fund's growth limitations, GPIQ has demonstrated that it can recover from declines and still ride the positive momentum of the market. For instance, GPIQ experienced a strong decline earlier in the year in response to the initial market shock following tariffs. The fund practically erased all of its prior gains in early April. Despite this, GPIQ was able to ride the positive momentum of the market in the following months, which demonstrated its ability to still provide capital appreciation over time.

While this may not seem like a big deal at first glance, I wanted to provide a quick comparison to some other popular high yield assets amongst retired investors. These assets are completely different in operating strategy and focus area so this isn't meant to be a one-to-one comparison. Instead, I want to demonstrate the specific risks that some of these other assets have:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): offers a comparable dividend yield of about 9.7% but generates its earnings from a portfolio of borrowers. ARCC is vulnerable to higher interest rates, which can lead to growing non-accruals from the pressure of thinner operating spreads. Additionally, ARCC's growth potential is fairly limited since business development companies are obligated to pay out 90% of their taxable income.
  • Enterprise Products Partners (EPD): offers a high dividend yield of 6.7% but is specifically focused on the midstream energy sector. This focus exposes investors to the sector-specific risks. Additionally, investors are exposed to single-stock risk here.
  • Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF): has a dividend yield of 18.2% but exposes investors to a portfolio of low rated credit investments, including high risk positions in CLOs (collateralized loan obligations). These higher yielding funds have a history of deteriorating investor capital over a longer holding period.

As we can see, GPIQ's approach is a lot more straightforward since it maintains diversity across constituents of the index. While there are still some risks, such as a vulnerability to a downturn within the tech sector, these risks are a lot less complex. I believe that investors can utilize GPIQ the same way that traditional index funds are utilized; deploying a simple dollar-cost average strategy over time can be efficient.

Tax-Efficient Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.4737 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.4%. Since the payouts are determined by the success of the written options, momentum of the market, and implied volatility levels of its holdings, the payout amounts can change every month. Despite the variable nature of payouts, the fund has done a great job at keeping payouts within a consistent range since its inception. Therefore, GPIQ has already proven to be an efficient buy-and-hold position for investors seeking income stability over time.

The only bit of weakness so far was demonstrated back in April of 2025. When markets were experiencing a sharp decline, the payout amount was slightly lower than its typically range. While the payouts eventually reverted back to its prior levels, this reveals that the consistency of distributions can be threatened during a prolonged decline. In a scenario where the fund's level of base assets decreases during a market pullback, there's a good chance that the payouts will also decline. I'd caution that since the fund's operating history is so limited, GPIQ still isn't time tested through a long downturn.

On a more positive note, the fund aims to pay out its distributions in a tax efficient manner to limit the tax liability for investors. This is another huge benefit for retirees that want to keep their tax burden low, while also receiving steady payouts. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 96.8% of November's payout was made up of return of capital. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

Just to help conceptualize how return of capital works, let's imagine that an investor buys a stock at $20 per share and it pays out a $2 per distribution using return of capital. Once the distribution is received, the investor's cost basis would be reduced down to $18 per share. So it is technically possible to maintain a position for long enough until your cost basis reaches $0. At this point, all distributions would be classified as long-term capital gains, which is still tax-efficient.

However, the terms 'tax-efficient' don't necessary mean that there will be no tax consequences that investors need to account for. Referring to the same section19(a) notice, portions of the payouts can be made up of net investment income, which have the highest tax burden for investors. So therefore, investors that want to absolutely minimize the tax impact from GPIQ should utilize a position in a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on GPIQ at this time due to the fund's ability to provide both capital appreciation and high income potential. Unlike other high income assets, GPIQ's index-linked approach means that investors can simply buy-and-hold while riding the momentum of the Nasdaq-100. This reduces the exposure to macro influences like interest rates or credit defaults. Additionally, the fund issues payouts in a tax-efficient manner, which ultimately reduces the tax liability for investors. This creates a greater level of flexibility in terms of what account types GPIQ can be utilized within.

data.title: Little Evidence Of Reshoring In Eurozone Businesses Despite Global Tensions
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands | Amrita Naik Nimbalkar, Junior Economist, Global Macro

Reshorings make up a negligible share of company restructurings in the eurozone

Import-dependent industries have been left increasingly vulnerable following events that have dominated the global stage over recent years – including the pandemic, conflict in the Middle East, and disruptions to supply chains in the wake of Russia's invasion of Ukraine. In turn, this has fuelled assumption that firms would bring production closer to home to reduce exposure to external shocks and has raised expectations of de-globalisation.

However, the European Restructuring Monitor (ERM) shows that reshorings among eurozone businesses remain limited for now. The ERM tracks restructuring announcements by medium-large-sized firms across member states, as reported in local media or company websites. While these medium-large firms represent only 1.1% of the total business in Europe, they employ more than half of the workforce and generate almost 70% of the value added. They are also generally more globalised than their smaller counterparts, making them an important contributor to industry trends. An event is included in the Monitor if it impacts at least 100 jobs or 10% of the workforce for companies with more than 250 people. The threshold does not apply to restoring, as it is difficult to predict the number of jobs added due to this. This makes reshoring likely to appear more frequently in the data, and it may therefore be overstated compared to offshoring to begin with.

The reshorings recorded are of two types: companies bringing back activities to their home country that were previously offshored to other EU or non-EU countries, and those reshoring to any EU country activities previously offshored to a non-EU country. Offshorings refer to moving activities of the company outside the country's borders, either to EU or non-EU countries. While this dataset will not capture all restructuring events since some will go unreported, it does provide an interesting insight into production trends.

Since 2019, the ERM has reported over 4500 restructuring events in the eurozone, such as business expansions, internal restructurings, closures, etc. Out of these, reshorings account for less than 1% (though likely overstated), and offshorings about 4%. Even in 2022, during the height of supply chain problems, transport costs going through the roof and shortages of inputs increasing, only 13 announcements of reshoring were traced by the Monitor. And while the numbers are low to begin with, the instances before the pandemic were, interestingly, actually higher, even compared to the offshorings. Since 2019, reported reshoring cases have fallen to a lower level than in 2016-2018.

Except for that period, offshoring cases have exceeded reshorings. For reshoring, data collection started in 2014, while for offshoring this goes back to 2002. Offshoring has moderated since the global financial crisis, but has not gone away altogether. Even in the post-pandemic years, with de-globalisation being widely discussed everywhere, we still see offshorings exceeding reshorings.

Offshorings have been more prominent than reshorings in the euro area

Restructurings in the eurozone, number of announcements recorded

Offshorings and reshorings have been concentrated in specific sectors and countries

Nearly half of all offshoring announcements have been from companies located in Western Europe – Germany, Belgium and France. The industries accounting for a majority of these were manufacturing, transport equipment, electrical and electronic products, and machinery. Almost 30% of the announcements since 2014 happened after Covid, suggesting that supply chain disruptions did not significantly discourage companies from offshoring. Several factors can explain why companies are still looking outside their borders, such as labour costs and shortages, catering more for local demand, large investments required for reshoring, regulatory ease elsewhere, or specialised and deeply integrated value chains which are difficult to modify.

Reshorings, while limited, have been prominent in France, Italy and Germany, in the sectors of manufacturing of electrical products, textiles and leather, food and beverage, and transport equipment. These industries account for nearly half of the announcements made since 2014.

Media coverage could be biased, of course, perhaps towards larger firms and more significant sectors, which might explain why these countries and sectors dominate. However, the underlying message remains clear – even among Europe’s largest countries and industries, there is little evidence of a structural shift towards reshoring, at least seen through this real-time dataset.

Western eurozone countries have a larger share of offshoring events

Despite limited evidence, the risk of trade fragmentation remains relevant

So, despite all the warning signs around a spread-out supply chain seen in recent years, there is still very limited evidence of actual reshoring activity to the eurozone. The costs of local production outweigh the risks of moving abroad for the majority of businesses.

Therefore, the main actions taken by European corporations seem to be the ringfencing of activity in countries perceived as higher risk and diversifying between locations for production. If production is brought closer, it is often more nearshoring than reshoring taking place. In the years ahead, geopolitical risks could add to reasons for producing more locally. But for now, the costs of producing in Europe itself seem to be too high to result in a widespread reshoring of activity.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Buying The 2024 Dip Was All Wrong (Downgrade)
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) was at around $84 when I started buying this stock in early 2025. After eleven months of 2025, it is difficult to argue that NVO had a very tough year as the stock is down by almost 50% year-to-date. This share price weakness was fair as the company continues losing ground to Eli Lilly (LLY), which we see from some latest developments. Losing competitive edge means deteriorated financial performance and less value for shareholders. Wall Street analysts continue downgrading Novo Nordisk's long-term EPS forecasts, meaning that the share price weakness is likely to persist. I do not want to hold this stock further and I am ready to recognize a quite painful 50% loss before the year-end. Therefore, NVO is a 'Sell' for me.

Fundamental analysis

NVO is down by almost 50% year-to-date, and this drop does not look like something unfair if we look at the company's deteriorating fundamentals. The company downgraded its EPS growth expectations a few times this year, which inevitably resulted in negative revisions from Wall Street. I don't have a screenshot with consensus EPS forecasts as of my previous thesis, but I was able to calculate them since I know that the share price was around $84 at that time and I had a screenshot with the forward P/E ratio forecast. As we can see, EPS downgrades for FY2025-2027 were double-digit and the magnitude of downgrade was larger for more remote years.

Such EPS revisions dynamic for NVO was not due to the unexpected weakness in obesity drugs (it was the company's primary growth driver in 2023-2024) but because NVO started losing ground in this crucial market. While NVO's share price has been tanking year-to-date, the main rival, Eli Lilly (LLY) was thriving with a 39% rally over the same period.

Unfortunately, I do not have a comparative table with LLY's EPS revisions compared to early 2025. However, we have the Earnings Revisions tab provided by SA that helps to track trend in revisions over the last six months. As we can see, LLY's EPS revisions were all green over the last half-a-year. While

Such a sharp contrast in EPS revisions between LLY and NVO is not explained by the cost side but by the revenue side. As we can see below, these two pharmaceutical giants were very close in terms of TTM revenue in early 2024 and the gap was not that big even by the end of 2024. However, this year has become a game changer as LLY's revenue growth accelerated while NVO's growth pace started decelerating (year-over-year growth: Q1=26%, Q2=20%, and Q3=12%). The fact that NVO's main rival's revenue is accelerating means that the company is losing ground. And the reason for such underperformance is simple and plain - NVO's flagship weight loss drugs are less effective compared to its rivals produced by LLY. Therefore, it is no wonder that LLY's Zepbound/Mounjaro pair of obesity drugs recently became the world's best-selling medicine.

In addition, Novo Nordisk's Ozempic pill recently failed to slow Alzheimer's disease progression in large trials. This development does not only mean longer path timewise to reach the rapidly growing multi-billion Alzheimer's disease market. It also means an opportunity cost of being one of the first movers because this niche is still not very crowded. Moreover, the recent failure means that the company might need to invest in this product more funds, which will weigh on profitability. Or they might admit that moving forward is not economically sound and it will be just R&D costs with zero returns. Anyway, the development is also a bearish one.

Valuation analysis

As a result of underperformance and guidance downgrades during 2025, NVO's forward P/E ratio setup has become quite bearish. The FY2025 estimated P/E of around 13 is relatively low. However, since the EPS is not expected to grow much, the P/E ratio is poised to stagnate. A forward P/E ratio of around 12 estimated for FY2028 does not look compelling for a company with stagnating profits, which is losing ground in the rapidly growing obesity drugs industry.

Besides the expected to stagnate forward P/E ratio, I also want to highlight NVO's high P/S ratio. Since it is not a revenue growth champion anymore, the stock is unlikely to deserve a premium P/S ratio. Compared to the sector median, NVO's P/S ratio is above the sector median. The gap between NVO and industry peers in terms of the Price/Book ratio is massive. Since NVO's assets are not expected to generate superior revenue and EPS growth, I think that the gap in Price/Book ratio has also potential to narrow over time.

Overall, I think that it is too easy to say that NVO is attractively valued even after the 50% year-to-date share price drop. As we see from very modest revenue and EPS growth projections from Wall Street, the share price weakness in 2025 is justified by the company's deteriorated long-term growth potential.

Of course, bulls would say that NVO's current share price is below consensus target price by around 25% at the moment. From this standpoint, I want to draw readers attention to the fact that NVO's actual price has been significantly below the average target price since mid-2024. But it did not help investors because multiple long-term down EPS revisions has led to the average target price crashing from around $150 to $56.

Mitigating factors

Novo Nordisk's history is rich, and the company has proven track-record of success. The company's gross profit margin is 83%, meaning that it has a large capacity to reinvest in marketing and R&D. Novo Nordisk's TTM R&D spending is $6.1 billion, which underscores the management's commitment to innovate. With such innovation-oriented culture, the company has potential to release new compelling products that will help it to return to the aggressive revenue and EPS growth path.

Due to strong profitability, the company's cash generating capacity is also solid with the TTM free cash flow of above $5 billion. The balance sheet is healthy, meaning that NVO can make positive surprises for shareholders like boosting the dividend or announcing a large buyback program. By the way, the TTM dividend yield is 3.6%, and some income investors might find it compelling. If the share price declines further, the dividend yield will go up, which might attract more dividend investors. This might be a positive catalyst for NVO that can potentially lead to a rally.

Conclusion

I think that NVO has potential to dip further because it continues losing ground to LLY and its recent failure in the Alzheimer's disease niche is also a painful one. I think that NVO is a 'Sell' at the moment, and I would better recognize almost a 50% loss and move one with the next investment idea.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Japanese Bond Rates Spike - Carry Trade Fears Are Overstated
data.description:
There has been quite a lot of press recently about the big shift in Japanese Bond Yields and the potential impacts inside and outside Japan.

Some speculate about the potential impact on the Yen carry trade, or fret about the increased volatility in one of the most stable global bond markets causing ripples in the global market.

I don't dismiss these fears at all, however I am looking to take advantage of the situation by allocating assets to the segment that I think might benefit from the root cause of the yield surge.

Japanese equities are poised to benefit from a big surge in Yen liquidity which is coming to the market as a result of recent policy announcements.

A straightforward way to play this without needing to select individual stocks, is via Japanese ETFs. I look at two options, iShares MCSI Japan ETF (EWJ) and iShares MSCI Japan Value ETF (EWJV) and select which to add to my portfolio.

What Happened

There was a short term squeeze in the yields of Japanese government bonds, as represented here by the 10 year bond or JGB. The JGB is the most relevant as it is used as the predominant duration in Japan for long bond benchmarking.

Over the course of a few days, the yield shot up from 1.63% to 1.8%, or about 10%. If you take a step back and look at the price movements over the last year, you can see that this is part of a year long trend, in which the rate has jumped from 1.1% to 1.8%. You can also see that there have been a number of equally dramatic surges in yields already this year, without all the fanfare.

The big drop in yields in April coincided with a 'risk off' market environment, as the major stock markets sold off. JGB yields plummeted from 1.6% to 1.1% in the flight to safety. As the market recovered from the sell-off, JGB prices fell back, and yields recovered.

So despite what you read in the media, the surge in yields

The absolute yield and the yield volatility are one aspect, another is to look at the spread between the USD and JPY 10 year yields. The chart below from worldgovernmentbonds.com shows the spread in basis points. This currently sits at 277bps, or 2.77%

This is the background for the famous Yen carry trade, in which investors borrow in cheap Yen, and invest in higher yielding assets. As you can see, the spread has been quietly unwinding all year, and had dropped from 3.5% to 2.5% before the recent push lower. It currently sits at around 2.2%

So the 'carry trade' drama seems to be a little overstated. I imagine that much of the carry trade has already eroded over the course of recent months. I doubt that a 2.45%-2.2% spread drop will create a dramatic unwind.

We can see that JGB yields have been quietly rising all year. Inflation in Japan is running at around 3%, so the yields have been artificially suppressed.

Why Bond Yields Spiked

The trigger for bond yields to spike was a massive $135bn economic stimulus package that was approved by the government.

This is a precursor to the first budget of new Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, the first ever female Prime Minister in Japan. Takaichi is a follower of a predecessor, Shinzo Abe, and his stimulative economic policies which were known in Japan as 'Abenomics'.

The agenda targets 17 strategic industries, lead by AI and Semiconductors, Defence, Transportation and Materials.

The bond market reaction seems fueled by concerns around further debt issuance from Japan, which is already leading the developed world with a whopping 255% of GDP.

Clearly the government hopes that the GDP improvements will reduce the debt to GDP ratio (as shown in this projection).

What This Means For Investors

US Investors And The USD

Most of us are not directly impacted by Japanese bond yields, being unlikely to either invest in, or borrow in Japanese Government Bonds.

The major concern for the markets in the US is that there is a significant amount of Yen carry trade broadly dispersed in US risk assets. The concern is that the trade has moved beyond a simple bond yield arbitrage play to a source of cheap credit for equity investments.

As discussed in this article, the problem is that there is no reliable estimate of the size of the trade. There are estimates of up to $1.7 trillion at the high end. What seems clear is that Japan has been a very large source of cheap liquidity at a scale that could be material to the global markets.

I think that the fear of a short term market impact is overblown. The carry trade, as discussed above, has been quietly unwinding, and this latest yield spike is unlikely to have massive shock waves.

However I do think that the longer term impact on global liquidity need to be considered. The chart below from Trading views, which measures M2 money supply in the US, Japan, China, Eurozone and the UK does show a contraction in liquidity in the early part of the year, however an increase in more recent months.

In my view, with the Yen yields having fallen during most of the year, carry trade impacts should already be part of the picture.

For the USD, the main driver is going to be US fiscal policy. This has been exerting downwards pressure on the US dollar index (DXY) for most of the year. There has been a period of recent strength. The big question is whether the administration will be successful in encouraging the Fed to prioritise rate cuts over its inflation mandate.

If rate cuts continue in the US faster than other major currencies, including the Yen, more USD weakness can be expected.

Japanese Equities

The biggest impact is likely to be felt in Japanese equities. The government is looking to directly add stimulus into the economy, by creating new liquidity.

Liquidity, as shown here by M2, money supply, is good for the prices of risk assets, particularly stocks.

Increasing Japanese Yen liquidity being targeted directly at supporting Japanese industry, is good for Japanese equities.

The real message in the news from Japan is 'Buy Japanese Equities'

The converse could be the case for the Japanese Yen. One concern around investing in foreign markets is currency risk.

The Bank Of Japan has a track record of active currency intervention, and has consistently defended the JPY at around 160 to the USD. With the price currently at around that level, ironically this might be a good time for investors to assume JPY risk.

How To Invest?

Stock picking in a foreign market is always tricky. You need to have a home advantage to be successful. There are some closed end funds that would claim to have this, but they can have quite concentrated portfolios. I think that the simplest approach is via ETFs.

Two that I want to look at are the iShares Japan ETF (EWJ) and the iShares value ETF (EWJV).

iShares MCSI Japan ETF (EWJ)

EWJ is a broad based Japan equity fund with 181 holdings. EWJ has $15.6bn in AUM, and has been around since 1996. It is highly liquid, with daily volume of 3.8m. The find is low beta, with a 3 year beta of 0.75%.

The fund holdings are modestly priced, with a PE ratio of 18x, and a price to book of 1.8x. Contrast this with the SPY PE of around 28x, and over 3x price to book.

The expense ratio is 0.5%.

The top holdings include some familiar names, such as Toyota and Sony, Hitachi and Nintendo, as well as some less well known names.

The sector allocation is heavy towards Industrials and Tech, Financial Services and Consumer Cyclical (Auto and Electronics.)

EWJ share price performance for the last 10 years has been steady, with a 62% rise over that period.

EWJ is a straightforward play on the Japanese market.

iShares MSCI Japan Value ETF (EWJV)

iShares also offers a value stock ETF - EWJV.

EWJV is much smaller, with a NAV of $500m and lower liquidity with a daily volume of 19,000. The expense ratio is only 0.15%

EWJV really does offer a low volatility low priced selection, with a beta of just 0.5x, a PE multiple of 13.5x, and a price to book of 1.28x.

The top portfolio holdings include some of the favourites like Toyota and Sony, but there's a bigger weighting to financials. Unsurprisingly, the IT names are not represented.

From a sector allocation point of view, the banks take over, with industrials next followed by consumer cyclicals.

Tech is less well represented, with just a 4% weighting compared to 18% in EWJ.

EWJV has significantly outperformed EWJ over the last decade on a total return basis, with a higher dividend yield having out stripped the price appreciation on EWJ.

Conclusion

Yield volatility in the JPY is nothing new - there has been quite some volatility in JGB rates in the past. The latest yield spike is far from unprecedented, and is part of a longer term trend.

Despite the 'Doom and Gloom' around the Yen carry trade, i think that this has been unwinding for some time now. The JPY/USD spread has been dropping for most of the year, I'd not be alarmed about this.

The Yen carry trade has been getting progressively less attractive all year as the JPY/USD yield spread has narrowed, overall global liquidity remains on the rise. Liquidity might be priced less attractively than before, but is abundant.

Looking at the root causes of the yield spike, the culprit is a stimulus package from the new Prime Minister, who plans to target spending to key industries.

This liquidity should be a positive for Japan equities broadly.

Comparing two iShares Japan ETFs, EWJ and EWJV, the past has favoured EWJV from a total return point of view. However, EWJ has a larger allocation to the industries most likely to benefit from the new stimulus package. While I normally favour low priced, low beta stocks, in this case I think that EWJ is likely to fare better.

I rate both EWJ and EWJV as Buys, but with a leaning towards EWJ.

data.title: Thrivent Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance factors

The Fund’s (TMBIX) outperformance in the 3rd quarter was largely due to its longer duration compared to the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. The Fund’s longer duration was also the main factor explaining the Fund’s underperformance for the trailing 12 months. In the 3rd quarter the Fund benefited from a roughly 30 basis point parallel yield curve shift to lower yields, with the strongest returns in the longer maturities. Rate moves were fueled by a weakening U.S. labor market with slower job growth, the unemployment rate trending higher, and unexpectedly large revisions to historical employment data. The housing market continues to navigate still high mortgage rates and anemic activity. Inflation continues to be slightly elevated, likely due in part to price increases from new tariffs. There was a slight increase in inflationary pressure, but not to the extent that some watchers feared back in April when the tariffs were first rolled out and the fear was that the overall tariff rate would be very high. With these factors in the economic background, the Fed decided that the time was right to cut the Fed Funds rate for the first time since December 2024.

The hospital sector was the strongest sector in the quarter, although it still lags for the trailing 12 months. The Fund benefited from its holdings of longer A-rated hospitals. The lagging sectors in the index for the 3rd quarter were tobacco and pre-refunded bonds. The tobacco sector suffered from weaker credit fundamentals while pre-refunded bonds lagged due to the sector’s shorter duration. Municipal credit has held up well and we are not seeing signs of impending wholesale weakness.

The municipal market had to digest heavy issuance in the first half of the year, however in the 3rd quarter there was a better balance between supply and demand. Municipal tax-exempt yields looked very attractive both relative to the past decade, and when compared to other fixed income such as Treasuries, corporates and taxable municipal securities. The 30-year municipal to Treasury ratio peaked at over 95% in the beginning of the quarter, but with tax-exempt municipal bonds strong performance the ratio tightened to around 89% at quarter end. This has still not returned to the lower 80%’s range the ratio was at the start of 2025. Adding to municipal securities attractiveness was the alleviation of the taxability threat to municipal debt with the passage of the tax bill.

Portfolio outlook

Longer maturity tax-exempt municipal bonds continue to look attractive both in absolute and relative terms. A better balance in supply and demand for the last quarter of 2025 should benefit the municipal market. The Federal Reserve is now on a path of cutting interest rates; two additional 25 basis point cuts are expected in the last quarter of the year. We would expect yield levels across the curve to continue to trend lower, especially if economic data confirms that inflation is not going to increase from the current levels. The fourth quarter began with a federal government shutdown and political uncertainty remains a concern for the markets and is likely to be a source of volatility going forward. We are actively monitoring our holdings, assessing each for potential impact of federal policy changes, including those affecting healthcare credits and higher education. Universities and colleges face headwinds with declining demographics, changing student visa regulations and political pressure. The Fund continues to seek out bonds where the obligors have a robust financial profile and provide the investors with a strong security package.

Performance

For the period ending September 30, 2025 • Periods less than one year are not annualized

Average annualized returns (%) 3 months YTD 1 year 3 years 5 years 10 years Since Inception
Thrivent Municipal Bond Fund-S share 3.35 0.88 -0.25 4.66 0.62 1.97 3.89
-Expense ratio: net 0.51%, gross 0.57%; Incept. date 10/31/1997
Bloomberg Muni Bd Index 3.00 2.64 1.39 4.74 0.86 2.34
Morningstar Muni National Long Avg 3.23 1.73 0.26 5.02 0.69 2.17

Top 10 Holdings (excluding derivatives and cash): 56.11% of Fund as of Aug 29 2025: Texas: 13.66%, New York: 8.98%, California: 5.88%, Florida: 5.16%, Illinois: 4.43%, Colorado: 4.16%, Pennsylvania: 3.99%, Ohio: 3.63%, Minnesota: 3.18%, Michigan: 3.04%

All data represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of the investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Call 800-847-4836 or visit thriventfunds.com for performance results current to the most recent month-end.

The Adviser has contractually agreed, for a period of one year from the date of the most recent prospectus, to waive certain fees and/or reimburse certain expenses associated with the Fund. If not waived, returns would have been lower. Refer to the Fees & Expenses table in the prospectus.

Any indexes shown are unmanaged and do not reflect the typical costs of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index. For more information on the index providers and their disclaimers, visit thriventfunds.com/privacy-and-security/index-provider-notices.

Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a market value-weighted index of investment grade municipal bonds with maturities of one year or more.

The Morningstar average represents the average total return annualized when greater than one year for all reported funds in the category. Morningstar averages do not include sales charges/fees. If included, returns would have been lower. ©2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Risks: Municipal bonds may be affected by political or economic conditions at the state, regional or federal level. Debt securities are subject to risks such as declining prices during periods of rising interest rates and credit risk, or the risk that an issuer may not pay its debt. Changes in federal income tax laws or rates may affect both the net asset value of the Fund and the taxable equivalent interest generated from securities in the Fund. Some issues may be subject to state and local taxes and/or the federal and state alternative minimum tax. The use of futures contracts involves additional risks such as a loss in value in the underlying instrument, which could decrease the Fund’s value. High yield securities are subject to increased credit risk as well as liquidity risk. The Adviser's assessment of investments may prove incorrect, resulting in losses or poor performance. When bond inventories are low in relation to the market size, there is the potential for decreased liquidity and increased price volatility. The Fund's value is influenced by the performance of the broader market. In unusual circumstances, the Fund could experience a loss when selling portfolio securities to meet redemption requests for a variety of reasons. These and other risks are described in the prospectus.
