data.title: Agora: A Classic Graham And Dodd Setup On Valuation
data.description:
Despite a couple of profitable quarters, Agora (API) is down about 6% since my last rating. I thought okay, what am I missing? Because in a market generally plagued by valuation concerns, the way it is pricing Agora seems at least a bit off.

A key concern in the past has been the fickle regulatory environment and customer base in China. While a cure to the regulatory environment is unlikely, the company’s dollar-based retention in the Shengwang unit suggests they are no longer losing ground the way they once did. Retention is not a cure-all, but it signals a business that has stopped sliding and is quietly rebuilding traction.

Inconsistent growth and profitability also made investors cautious in the past. But the company achieved its fourth consecutive quarter of GAAP profitability and revenue growth seems to be reaching for a double-digit number. While it will be competitive, the company’s conversational AI offering is also a clear catalyst that can be break the cycle of inconsistent growth. After a detailed analysis of the company's valuation I see the stock as fundamentally mispriced, so I am upgrading it to “Strong Buy”.

Q3 Earnings

Q3 revenues were up 12% year-over-year at $35.4 million, and the guidance is for a 7%-10% growth in the last quarter. The guidance is interesting as it overlaps with 4Q24, the quarter when the turnaround began. The fact that revenue is rising even as sales and marketing spend fell 5.6% suggests customers are adopting or renewing with less push from the company, which means the value proposition is likely resonating. While management did not provide precise dollar amounts, they did mention conversational AI usage expanded 150% quarter-over-quarter and is likely to be a sizable contributor towards the end of 2026.

The more interesting story is around the company’s operating expenses (or, opex) as they plunged 44.8% year-over-year to $25.3 million. Even sequentially opex as a percentage of sales was down 590 basis points. Much of it is personnel-related which make them sustainable into the next year and improves management’s ability to translate incremental revenue to earnings. Important in terms of aligning with shareholder interest, about $13.4 million of the decline is due to stock-based compensation. Taken together, the combination of tighter cost discipline and steadier revenue puts Agora in a position where even modest top line growth can produce meaningful operating leverage.

Valuation and Risk-pricing

Now the data feeds on Seeking Alpha which are likely automated, show that the company’s EV is $229.33 million. From a strict accounting sense and considering a one-day lag that would be correct.

Accounting EV ($226.78 mn) = Market Cap ($322.98 mn) – Cash ($169.9 mn) + Debt ($73.7 mn)

But in a practical investment analysis point of view, you have to deduct the company’s long-term bank deposits. The goal with EV is to see what the company would cost to acquire debt-free. In a takeover scenario, an acquirer would see this as excess capital that can be liquidated to offset the purchase price. For example, a 2-year bank deposit is extremely liquid. You may lose a month’s interest for breaking it early, but the principal is safe. Agora isn’t a bank either. So apart from generating interest from a large cash pool, there likely isn’t an operational reason to invest cash.

EV ($17.78 mn) = Market Cap ($322.98 mn) – Cash ($169.9 mn) + Debt ($73.7 mn) – Long-term cash equivalents ($209 mn)

(Millions of USD) Amount
Revenue Jan - Sep 2025 102.9
Revenue Q4 Expected 37.5
FY25 Expected Sales 140.4

After adjusting for long-term cash equivalents, we get an EV of $17.78 million. If you divide that by 2025 expected sales, we get an EV/Sales ratio of 0.13. To put it bluntly it seems like the market is pricing the company as if it were going out of business. Given recent results, that can’t be right. In my experience I have never heard of even distressed tech company’s trading at these multiples. So again, I thought, what am I missing?

Going back to first principles, the only reason the math works out like this is because of the cash. So, if the cash were to be trapped because of property rights in China that would make some sense. And Agora’s annual report actually alludes to that risk in as many words. So we must write off the valuation associated with that risk because the market clearly wants us to. Further into discussions of its credit risk, the report lists where the cash equivalents are held.

Earlier in the year, 39% and 23% of $370.3 million was held in US and Singapore respectively. That leaves about $137 million in Hong Kong and China. Adding that back gives us an EV of $154.78 million and EV/Sales 1.10. That is more in line for a company in decline. However, the delta on the expected EV for a similar company and the actuals can be attributed to market mispricing. I was still very curious (not convinced to put it bluntly) about the evaluation of credit risk as that was my bread and butter for a brief period of my professional career.

Agora used a Variable Interest Entity (or, VIE) structure before January 2025. In a VIE, the company does not technically own the Chinese operating business. Instead, it has a contractual arrangement saying “we own the profits” through a service fee without holding the bank accounts directly. If the Chinese government decides to block capital outflows, it would be difficult to wire that cash out of Shanghai. However, in January 2025, the Shanghai Shengwang became a 100% subsidiary or a Wholly Foreign-Owned Enterprise (or, WFOE). Under this arrangement, wiring that cash out of China becomes a standard issue of capital controls and not property rights. This effectively means that the company can wire that cash through two means – a dividend or a capital reduction.

The standard withholding tax in China is 10% but with Hong Kong there’s a double taxation treaty that reduces it to 5%. A capital reduction or a return of capital has a 0% tax rate but let’s assume they don’t do that. In effect, the haircut on the cash is 5% and let’s add 5% for any other frictions that might exist moving that amount of capital. We still get an EV that is around $32 million.

The last question to which I had no answer to is - why is the management not taking the company private? There are several instances of China exposure companies at distressed multiples eventually being taken private (Chindata and Changyu). And may be that reinforces the bull case, but it doesn’t answer why the company still trades in public markets.

Conclusion

Agora is a classic Graham and Dodd setup where the cash balance covers the price of the stock. At the current price, the market is offering a real time engagement company at the price of the furniture in its office. I am therefore, upgrading my rating to "Strong Buy".

data.title: ZIM Integrated Shipping: Rich FQ4'25 Payouts Unlikely - Painful New Normal Ahead
data.description:
I previously covered ZIM Integrated Shipping (ZIM) in August 2025, discussing how its H2'25 prospects appeared rather underwhelming, with the moderating Drewry's World Container Index likely bringing forth an impacted top/bottom-line performance, disappointing dividend payouts, and mixed share price performance.

This was worsened by the overly discounted take private offer, with the ridiculously low $20 price per share offer presenting its mixed near-term prospects if the takeover succeeded.

In this article, I shall discuss why ZIM remains a Hold, attributed to the spot price risks arising from the return of shipping traffic through the Suez Canal and the projected capacity oversupply through 2027 - with the outsized FQ3'25 performance/dividend payouts unlikely to be replicated in FQ4'25.

ZIM's Execution Remains Excellent Despite Numerous Headwinds

ZIM 1-Y Stock Price

The bulls have proven my last Hold rating wrong indeed, as ZIM outperforms with a double digits total return profile thanks to the highly competent fleet renewal cadence at a time of volatile spot prices, container ship oversupply, tariff impact, and intensifying geopolitical tensions.

For example, the management has made the strategic choice to "redeliver (chartered) vessels to owners," as observed in its notably moderating fleet capacity of 709K TEU in FQ3'25 (-7.5% QoQ/-9.1% YoY), with a further downtrend trend likely.

This is because ZIM has also hinted at the possibility of redelivery of 3 vessels worth 5.6K TEU in FQ4'25 and another 17 vessels worth 55K TEU in FY2026, if the demand profile and/or spot prices do not improve - all of which may trigger tailwinds to its near-term performance attributed to the improved flexibility in responding to the "changing market conditions or shifts in our commercial strategy":

It is likely that we will return and continue to return vessels that are coming up for renewal and focusing on, at the end of the day, continue to operate the larger ship, the more efficient tonnage, the newer and greener capacity that we have received over the course of the past couple of years...

And we need to acknowledge that the charter market is still to date elevated. So it's expensive to recharter tonnage at a time when the revenue per TEU carried is under pressure. I think for as long as this situation continues, it is more likely than not that we will redeliver the vessels that come up for renewal as opposed to trying to recharter them. (Seeking Alpha)

The Volatile Drewry's World Container Index

Most importantly, given that 60% of its current fleet capacity are newbuilds and 40% are LNG powered, it is unsurprising that ZIM has been able to command a sequentially improved contracted pricing of $1.6K per TEU in FQ3'25 (+8.8% QoQ/-35.4% YoY) despite the notably lower spot prices of approximately $2.28K per TEU in Q3'25 (-13.9% QoQ/-52.5% YoY).

Combined with the lower operating expenses/cost of services, it is unsurprising that the company has reported a sequentially expanded adj EBIT margin of 14.6% in FQ3'25 (+5.5 points QoQ/-30.1 YoY) and adj net income margin of 6.9% (+5.5 points QoQ/-33.8 YoY).

This development underscores their ability to remain profitable despite the uncertain market environment - with it naturally lending credence to the outsized stock price recovery by +35.8% from the October 2025 bottom.

It is unsurprising then that ZIM has been able to consequently offer an outsized variable dividend payout of $0.31 in FQ3'25 (+416.6% QoQ/-88.9% YoY) based on the company's dividend policy at 30% of Q1/Q2/Q3 net incomes and 50% of Q4 net incomes.

As a commodity/dividend-oriented stock, the rich payouts have been extremely encouraging at a time of market uncertainties, since the positive adj net incomes have been directly accretive to its shareholder return program.

This reason is also why I am of the opinion that ZIM is likely to outperform in the long-term, aided by the cost savings observed from the ongoing fleet resizing and the transition to a more efficient tonnage profile as they respond to the rapidly changing operating environment with more "agility."

Despite my prior concerns surrounding its balance sheet, the management has been reporting a relatively stable net leverage ratio of 0.9x in FQ3'25 (inline from a year ago) and net debt level of $2.64B (+1.8% YoY).

These numbers exemplify ZIM's highly competent execution and excellent ability to service its debt/lease obligations over the next few years of uncertainties, with patient investors likely to continue enjoying rich payouts once in a while.

ZIM Remains A Hold Here

ZIM 3-Y Stock Price

Given the outsized stock price gains, I am of the opinion that ZIM is likely to lose part of the upward momentum attributed to the potential selling pressure after the ex-dividend date of December 01, 2025.

This is especially since the management has hinted at "continued pressure on freight rates during the remainder of the fourth quarter and into 2026," attributed to the projected oversupply through 2027:

The outlook for container shipping remains cautious as growth in supply is expected to outpace the growth in demand in the foreseeable future. The order book has continued to grow and now stands at 31%.

While the growth in supply is expected to slow down in 2026 when we compare to 2025, deliveries are projected to surge again in 2027 to more than 3 million TEUs of capacity, exceeding the record set in 2024. (Seeking Alpha)

At the same time, readers may want to note that the ongoing traffic return through the Suez Canal arising from the ceasefire is likely to trigger notably higher excess container ship capacity and lower tonnage miles, with it likely triggering further downward pressure on the spot prices.

Based on ZIM's YTD adj EBIT performance of $872M (-53.8% YoY) and the management's raised FY2025 adj EBIT guidance of $800M at the midpoint (-68.6% YoY), it is apparent that FQ4'25 may bring forth minimal profitability and consequently, dividend payouts.

While the company may have reported an outsized FQ3'25 performance and the higher than expected dividend payouts, I am of the opinion that the same numbers are unlikely to be replicated in FQ4'25 - with it underscoring why I am reiterating my prior caution surrounding the risks of chasing variable payouts.

While existing investors may continue holding while enjoying the rich FQ3'25 payouts, I do not recommend anyone to add at current levels, attributed to its likely to be lumpy performance in FQ4'25/FY2026.

ZIM is a Hold here.
